intro: Transitioning from JavaScript to Scala shifts execution from a dynamically typed, single-threaded event loop environment to a statically typed, highly concurrent JVM ecosystem. Developers typically make this move to leverage Scala's powerful functional programming paradigms, strong compile-time safety, and superior performance for massive parallel processing. This migration replaces prototype-based object orientation with a sophisticated blend of object-oriented and functional constructs backed by advanced type inference.

diffs.from: Dynamically typed with weak coercion; types are bound to values at runtime, often requiring defensive checks.
diffs.to: Statically typed with sophisticated type inference, structural typing, implicits, and compile-time verification.

diffs.from: C-style syntax relying heavily on functions, object literals, and prototypes, with optional semicolons.
diffs.to: Expression-oriented syntax blending object-oriented classes/traits with functional patterns, pattern matching, and minimal boilerplate.

diffs.from: Managed via a V8/runtime-specific generational garbage collector tied to the host environment thread.
diffs.to: Managed by the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) garbage collector, optimized for high-throughput heap allocations across multiple threads.

diffs.from: Single-threaded event loop utilizing Promises, async/await keywords, and microtask queues for non-blocking I/O.
diffs.to: Multi-threaded concurrency leveraging Futures, Akka/Pekko actors, or functional effect systems (ZIO, Cats Effect) backed by ThreadPools.

diffs.from: Minimalist core library heavily reliant on third-party ecosystem (NPM) for advanced data structures and utilities.
diffs.to: Comprehensive, highly optimized collections framework featuring native immutable/mutable structures and powerful functional combinators.

examples.title: Asynchronous Data Fetching
examples.description: Converting an asynchronous API fetch routine using async/await to Scala's Future with an explicit ExecutionContext.

examples.title: Data Transformation with Collection Utilities
examples.description: Filtering and mapping a list of objects based on a property matching operation.
examples.toCode:
case class Order(id: String, status: String, amount: Double)
case class ProcessedOrder(id: String, total: Double)

def processOrders(orders: List[Order]): List[ProcessedOrder] = {
  orders
    .filter(_.status == "completed")
    .map(o => ProcessedOrder(o.id, o.amount * 1.1))
}

tips.title: Embrace Immutability By Default
tips.body: Replace JavaScript 'let' and mutable object mutations with Scala 'val' and case class 'copy' methods to eliminate side effects.

tips.title: Leverage Options Instead of Nulls
tips.body: Avoid checking for 'null' or 'undefined'. Use Scala's 'Option[T]' (Some/None) along with pattern matching or 'getOrElse' constructs.

tips.title: Understand the ExecutionContext
tips.body: While JS manages its event loop implicitly, Scala's Futures require an explicit or implicit ExecutionContext to schedule threads.

tips.title: Shift from Prototypes to Traits
tips.body: Translate runtime prototype compositions into Scala's compile-time mixin composition using 'traits' for shared, reusable interfaces.

faqs.question: Can Scala code run in the browser like JavaScript?
faqs.answer: Yes, through Scala.js, which compiles Scala source code directly into optimized JavaScript, allowing sharing of logic between frontend and JVM backend.

faqs.question: How do JavaScript objects map to Scala?
faqs.answer: JavaScript plain objects map best to Scala Case Classes for typed data, or to 'Map[String, Any]' if the keys and values are fully dynamic.

faqs.question: What is the equivalent of JS undefined in Scala?
faqs.answer: There is no direct structural equivalent. Scala models missing values safely using 'None' via the 'Option' monad instead of unassigned reference types.
